query: histology of endometriotic lesions and correlation with estrogen levels

retrieved_docs.title: Apoptosis -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Menstrual Cycle
retrieved_docs.content: The sloughing of the inner lining of the uterus (the endometrium) after the withdrawal of estrogen and progesterone in the menstrual cycle is a physiological process of apoptosis. [29]

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Summarize the appropriate evaluation of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Outline the management options available for premalignant lesions of the endometrium. retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal therapy resistance is seen in nearly 30% of patients. This is thought to be due to a decreased availability of progesterone receptors and alteration of the apoptotic signaling pathway of the endometrial glandular cells. [21] [15] Additional factors that may be responsible include the down-regulation of progesterone receptor and activation of the transforming growth factor signaling pathway. Less likely, resistance to hormonal therapy could result from mutations in PR or possibly paracrine effects. The histologic response of the glands of AEH/EIN is strongly coupled to the decidual response in the stroma, so the possibility of a paracrine effect is plausible, but the epithelial-stromal interactions of the endometrium are incompletely understood. [21] [15] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of endometriomas is the same as endometriosis in general since endometriomas are a subset of this more extensive medical condition. The pathway for this disease process occurs with the hormonal response of the ectopic endometrial tissue. This tissue responds to the cyclical hormonal changes of a woman’s menstrual cycle in the same way that the intrauterine endometrium does. It will become proliferative, secretory, and slough just as it would if it were within the uterus. With these fluctuations comes varying concentrations of cytokines and prostaglandin molecules.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The role of steroid hormones in the pathogenesis of endometriosis is also indisputable. Estrogen is the main hormone responsible for the propagation and expansion of the endometrium. The increased action of aromatase, mainly in the deep infiltrating endometriosis, leads to locally increased estrogen activity. [10] On the other hand, the inability of progesterone to have an antagonistic action with estrogen in the endometrial tissue is a determining factor for establishing endometriosis. [12] The epigenetic changes are also an undeniable factor in the pathogenesis of endometriosis, and this is demonstrated by the fact that not only women of reproductive age suffer from the disease but also adolescents and younger women with a family history. [13] Specific genetic loci have also been identified for the initiation of the disease, making some women more prone to the disease than others. However, the genetic profile is not yet fully understood. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Endometriosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histologic examination of cutaneous endometriosis typically reveals intradermal endometrial glands lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium within a fibrovascular stroma. The presence of extravasated red blood cells often aids in diagnosis. Immunohistochemical staining commonly shows glandular epithelial cells positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors, while stromal cells may express CD10.

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Histopathology -- Postmenopausal Endometrial Histologic Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoestrogenic environment has several effects on the postmenopausal endometrium that are apparent on microscopic examination. Normal premenopausal proliferative endometrium contains regularly spaced, ordered glands lined by simple epithelial cells within the stroma with an approximate 1 to 1 gland-to-stroma ratio. Furthermore, histologically, mitotic activity is a characteristic feature of proliferative endometrium. Conversely, normal histologic findings in the postmenopausal endometrium show widely spaced glands that vary from small to cystically dilated. Moreover, the mitotic proliferative activity characteristic of a premenopausal endometrium is not apparent in postmenopausal endometrial cells. [8] [4] Pathologic endometrial findings have varying histologic findings depending on the underlying etiology. For instance, the histopathology of endometrial polyps frequently shows cellular immaturity with cystic hyperplasia, while smooth muscle fibers are a characteristic finding of uterine fibroids. Both conditions have a higher risk of malignant transformation in postmenopausal women. [16] [17] In women with PMB, the most concerning abnormal histologic findings are endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometritis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Conversely, chronic endometritis is characterized by endometrial infection by microorganisms not necessarily associated with concurrent cervical or vaginal colonization. The microbial infection leads to an immune response and chronic inflammation, with significant infiltrates of endometrial stromal plasmacytes and the development of micropolyps. [5] [23] Elevations in interleukin-1b and tumor necrosis factor-alpha are also present, increasing estrogen synthesis in endometrial glandular cells. This increased estrogen synthesis may be associated with micropolyps, often seen on hysteroscopic evaluation in patients diagnosed with chronic endometritis. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Receptivity -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Estrogen dominance results in increased cell proliferation, inflammation, and angiogenesis, exacerbating the non-receptive endometrial state. In studies of endometriosis, a condition defined by excess estrogen and progesterone resistance, it has been shown that the resulting inflammation of the endometrium is associated with an overexpression of p450 aromatase, which changes the dynamic of progesterone-to-estrogen activity. The overexpression of aromatase has been associated with poor IVF outcomes. [24] Endometrial receptivity continues to be a very active area of research. The contributing factors to endometrial receptivity have not all been recognized and continue to be investigated.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: For radiological evaluation, radiology technicians conduct ultrasound exams on the patients, while radiologists interpret the images. The lab and imaging results communicated to the primary care provider allows the diagnostic picture to come together. If surgery is the chosen treatment, the obstetrician/gynecologist and the surgical team manage the patient. Nurses care for the patients in the outpatient environment, as well as the pre- and post-operative settings. Medical management involves pharmacists who dispense the hormone therapies to the patient and answer any drug-related questions.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The precursor lesion of type 1 EC is AH. The WHO classification of endometrial hyperplasia includes two major categories, hyperplasia and atypical hyperplasia/endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia (AH/EIN). [16] While simple hyperplasia has a wide range of appearances, including an increased gland-to-stroma ratio and dilated glands; these lesions rarely progress to EC (1% to 3%). They are caused by persistent estrogen stimulation and may evolve into cystic atrophy when estrogen is withdrawn. AH/EIN shows proliferating glands with nuclear atypia.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Cancer (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrioid cancer develops in response to the exposure of endometrium to unopposed estrogen, which leads to hyperplasia and then dysplasia of the endometrial glands and hence is referred to as adenocarcinoma. Multiple studies have demonstrated a strong association between intrinsic factors that cause increased serum estrogen, e.g., obesity and type II diabetes mellitus with endometrial adenocarcinoma. [9] [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Estrogen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Uterus: In the uterus, estrogen helps proliferate endometrial cells in the follicular phase of the menstrual cycle, thickening the endometrial lining in preparation for pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Premalignant lesions of the endometrium are a characteristic sequence of atypical endometrial hyperplasia or intraepithelial neoplasia that may give rise to the two main types of endometrial carcinomas. Type 1 occurs in estrogen predominance and/or progesterone insufficiency state and resembles proliferative endometrium. Type 2 is the serous type of endometrial carcinoma normally seen with postmenopausal endometrial atrophy, where mutation of P53 leads to intraepithelial carcinoma and progression. Type 1, on the other hand, harbors the inactivation of PTEN and other pathways. The presenting symptoms for premalignant lesions are menorrhagia and metrorrhagia (type 1) and postmenopausal bleeding (type 2). Management of premalignant lesions includes hysterectomy (total hysterectomy, not supracervical hysterectomy). In selected younger patients wanting to have children, medical management with high-dose gestagen therapy with appropriate close histological monitoring may be done. Untreated, almost all premalignant lesions of endometrium would progress to invasive carcinoma over a period of a few years. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients suffering from premalignant lesions of the endometrium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone, a synthetic progestogenic compound, may be used to treat انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometrial hyperplasia). By supplying progestin activity, 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone counteracts estrogen‑driven proliferation of the uterine lining, helping to normalize endometrial thickness and lower the risk of progression to malignancy. This therapeutic application highlights the role of hormonal modulation in managing intrauterine disorders and underscores the importance of appropriate hormonal therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 376
retrieved_docs.content: Reproductive System Disorder. Endometriosis exhibits a broader relationship with اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث and is linked to اضطراب رحمي through an inverse_isa relationship, indicating that uterine disorder is the more general class encompassing endometriosis. Overall, these relationships depict a multi‑layered network in which sexual function disorders, obstetric complications, and gynecologic conditions intersect. Recognizing that Female Reproductive System Disorder resides at the intersection of خلل الوظيفة الجنسية, pregnancy disorders such as اضطراب الحمل and مخاض قبل الأوان, and uterine pathology provides clinicians with a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, interdisciplinary management, and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 477
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث. Benign vaginal tumor (ورم مهبلي حميد) is another entity classified within Female Reproductive System Disorder. Collectively, these entities—sexual dysfunction, preterm labor, infertility, uterine cancer, endometriosis, and benign vaginal tumor—are interrelated components of the Female Reproductive System Disorder taxonomy, reflected in major regulatory terminologies and linked by clinical relationships that support integrated diagnostic, therapeutic, and regulatory strategies in reproductive medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is connected to endometriosis through a broader relationship, indicating that endometriosis falls within the scope of disorders affecting the female reproductive system. Both Female Reproductive System Disorder and endometriosis are classified under the parent category اضطراب رحمي, which translates to “uterine disorder.” In this hierarchy, اضطراب رحمي serves as the overarching class, with Female Reproductive System Disorder representing a broader group of conditions and endometriosis representing a specific type of uterine disorder. This structure highlights how endometriosis is a particular manifestation of uterine pathology within the larger context of female reproductive system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure whose site is the uterus, indicating that it is performed on or involves the uterine organ. Endometriosis is a disease that is located in the uterus, reflecting the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within or on the uterine structure. Both concepts highlight the uterus as a central focus for therapeutic interventions and pathological conditions, underscoring the importance of uterine health in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: According to the finding_site_of relationship, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are identified with a finding site in the ovarium. In a similar manner, a Lesion of ovary is described as having a has_finding_site in the ovarium. The ovarium itself is linked by a has_finding_site to اضطراب مبيضي (an ovarian disorder) and by a has_location to حمل مَبيضِيّ (an ovarian pregnancy). These interconnected statements highlight that inflammatory conditions, lesions, and specific disorders of the ovary are all centered on the ovary as the primary anatomical site, and they may be associated with rare presentations such as ovarian pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing the ovary’s role in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2877
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is classified as a type of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine/endometrial disorders are classified as a subset within the broader category of enlarged uterine leiomyoma tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), indicating that conditions affecting the uterus and endometrium are encompassed by the pathology of large fibroid growths. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing fibroid enlargement as a central component in the spectrum of uterine/endometrial disease and suggests that assessment of fibroid size and impact is a key aspect of managing patients with uterine/endometrial disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate is indicated as a possible treatment for menopausal disorders, while synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are each identified as potential therapies for alleviating menopausal symptoms. These estrogenic agents represent therapeutic options that can be employed to manage the various clinical manifestations associated with menopause, providing clinicians with multiple pharmacologic choices to address both the broader disorder and its specific symptomatic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with other excision of cervix and uterus, and it is also clinically associated with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ (endometritis). These connections indicate that women who are pregnant within the uterus may have a history of surgical removal of cervical or uterine tissue and may be prone to, or present with, inflammation of the endometrial lining. Recognizing these relationships is important for obstetric care, as prior cervical or uterine excision can influence pregnancy management, and the presence of endometritis can affect maternal and fetal outcomes, necessitating vigilant monitoring and appropriate treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesions of the ovary, and noninflammatory disorders of the ovary all share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location for these diverse pathologic processes. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical site of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), an ectopic gestation that occurs within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the ovary in both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory pelvic conditions as well as in rare pregnancy complications, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of ovarian involvement in female pelvic health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs are identified as having the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location where such pelvic inflammatory conditions manifest. Likewise, any ovary problem is described as having its finding site in the ovary itself, reinforcing the ovary’s central role in the pathology of disorders that originate in this organ. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is noted to be the anatomical location for an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), highlighting that ectopic implantation of a gestational sac can occur within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize the ovary’s significance as both the site of inflammatory pelvic disease, the focal point for ovary‑specific problems, and a possible location for an ovarian pregnancy, all of which are important considerations in diagnosis and management of female pelvic health.
